title: Arkansas
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Arkansas, most people who are incarcerated become eligible for parole release—called “transfer to community correction”—after serving minimum terms of one-third or one-half of their judicial maximum sentences, which may be reduced by “meritorious good time” credits. Sorting into the one-third and one-half categories is based on the seriousness determination made by the Sentencing Commission. Substantial changes in release law will take effect in 2025 under Act 659 (2023).²

title: Arkansas
text:
In Prison Past Parole Eligibility
Most recent data shows that 7 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, another 10 percent will reach parole eligibility next year, and an additional 83 percent will reach parole eligibility in more than one year.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 81 percent. Our forecasting model projects that the percentage of people past their initial parole eligibility will decrease by 1 percent.

title: Arkansas
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration rates past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Parole Release: This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: West Virginia
text:

Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. In West Virginia, indeterminate sentences carry minimum and maximum terms that are specified in statute and cannot be varied by the court when imposing a prison sentence. title: West Virginia
text:
Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.³ (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 50 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Data is excluded for years where more than 10 percent of the relevant cases are missing the admission year or the max. sentence length variables, and for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases areare missing PEY. Relevant cases are all except those with life sentences, sentences of less than a year, and admission types that are not new court commitments or not available. All figures are calculated among relevant cases only.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁵

Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.³ (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (25 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). Third, in South Carolina there is no parole for people with two or more violent offenses. Because data on previous terms was not published for 2018 and 2020, rules-based estimated PEY was removed for every one with a violent offense. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁵

title: South Carolina
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration rates past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Parole Release: This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. The parole board has broad discretion to set minimum terms to parole release consideration for each prisoner individually. Violent offenses are subject to an 85-percent eligibility rule.²

title: Montana
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration rates past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Parole Release: This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Massachusetts
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Massachusetts, sentencing courts decide the length of minimum terms in relation to judicial maximum sentences without limitations. Credit-based deductions from both minimum and maximum terms are statutorily capped at 35 percent.²

title: Massachusetts
text:
Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.³ (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (100 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 100 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. title: Kentucky
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration rates past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Parole Release: This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Georgia
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Georgia, people who are incarcerated become eligible for discretionary parole release after serving one-third of their judicial maximum sentences, but no earlier than after 9 months and no later than after 7 years.²

Most recent data shows that 69 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, another 20 percent will reach parole eligibility next year, and an additional 11 percent will reach parole eligibility in more than one year.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 13 percent. Our forecasting model projects that the percentage of people past their initial parole eligibility will remain around 68 percent.

title: Georgia
text:
Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.³ (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (33 percent). Second, the absolute min. sentence of 9 months when the most serious offense is non-violent and 7 yearsfor people with offenses of more than 21 years when the most serious offense is violent is computed. The previously computed rules-based estimate was replaced by the absolute min. sentence if the latter was longer. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁵

title: Key Findings
text:
Summary:
This section calculates the average number of years individuals spent in prison past their parole eligibility year by race and ethnicity. The analysis excludes states with unreliable or missing data and states that abolished discretionary parole to ensure results are robust and representative.
Finding:

Code
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
# Years Spent in Prison Past Parole Eligibility
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
# Combine excluded states
combined_excluded_states <- unique(c(
  states_with_high_missing$state,
  states_with_high_missing_race$state,
  states_to_exclude
))

# Remove states that abolished parole
valid_states <- setdiff(
  ncrp_yearendpop_filtered$state, 
  unique(c(combined_excluded_states, states_abolished_parole$state))
)

# Filter NCRP data to valid states only
ncrp_yearendpop_filtered <- fnc_filter_pe_population_criteria(
  data = ncrp_yearendpop_filtered,
  exclude = states_to_exclude,
  dont_filter = states_nofilter
)

# Filter NCRP data to people currently eligible for parole (also past parole eligibility since it's yearend)
ncrp_past_pe <- ncrp_yearendpop_filtered |>
  filter(state %in% valid_states, parelig_status == "Current")

# Average time served past PE for people still in prison by race and ethnicity and state
avg_past_pe_race_by_state <- ncrp_past_pe |>
  filter(race != "Unknown") |>
  filter(
    state %in% states_use_other_race_eth$state |
      (!state %in% states_use_other_race_eth$state &
         race %in% c("White, non-Hispanic", "Hispanic, any race", "Black, non-Hispanic"))
  ) |>
  mutate(years_to_estimated_pey = abs(years_to_estimated_pey)) |>
  group_by(state, race, rptyear) |>
  summarise(
    avg_years_to_estimated_pey = mean(years_to_estimated_pey, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_years_past_pe = sum(years_to_estimated_pey, na.rm = TRUE),
    people = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  fnc_filter_by_year(which_overall_year)

# Calculate weighted averages
avg_past_pe_race <- avg_past_pe_race_by_state |>
  group_by(race) |>
  summarise(
    avg_years_to_estimated_pey = sum(avg_years_to_estimated_pey * people, na.rm = TRUE) / sum(people, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_years_past_pe = sum(total_years_past_pe, na.rm = TRUE),
    people = sum(people, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Get states used
included_states <- unique(avg_past_pe_race_by_state$state)
states_using_2018_data <- avg_past_pe_race_by_state |>
  filter(rptyear == 2018) |>
  pull(state) |>
  unique()

# Generate Text Outputs
cat("In 2019, ",
    highlight_number(comma(avg_past_pe_race$people[avg_past_pe_race$race == "White, non-Hispanic"])),
    "White, non-Hispanic individuals collectively spent",
    highlight_number(comma(round(avg_past_pe_race$total_years_past_pe[avg_past_pe_race$race == "White, non-Hispanic"], 1))),
    "years in prison past their parole eligibility year across",
    highlight_number(length(included_states)), "states, serving an average of",
    highlight_number(round(avg_past_pe_race$avg_years_to_estimated_pey[avg_past_pe_race$race == "White, non-Hispanic"], 1)),
    "years beyond parole eligibility.<br><br>")

In 2019, 82,537 White, non-Hispanic individuals collectively spent 248,020 years in prison past their parole eligibility year across 24 states, serving an average of 3 years beyond parole eligibility.

Code
cat("In 2019, ",
    highlight_number(comma(avg_past_pe_race$people[avg_past_pe_race$race == "Black, non-Hispanic"])),
    "Black, non-Hispanic individuals collectively spent",
    highlight_number(comma(round(avg_past_pe_race$total_years_past_pe[avg_past_pe_race$race == "Black, non-Hispanic"], 1))),
    "years in prison past their parole eligibility year across",
    highlight_number(length(included_states)), "states, serving an average of",
    highlight_number(round(avg_past_pe_race$avg_years_to_estimated_pey[avg_past_pe_race$race == "Black, non-Hispanic"], 1)),
    "years beyond parole eligibility.<br><br>")

In 2019, 74,824 Black, non-Hispanic individuals collectively spent 280,422 years in prison past their parole eligibility year across 24 states, serving an average of 3.7 years beyond parole eligibility.

title: Key Findings
text:
By Offense
Summary:
This section analyzes the offense types of individuals held past their parole eligibility year, distinguishing between violent and nonviolent offenses. Nonviolent offenses are further categorized into Property, Drug, and Public Order offenses.
Finding:

Code
# Categorize offenses into violent vs. nonviolent, keeping "Unknown" offenses for initial analysis
ncrp_past_pe_offense_initial <- ncrp_past_pe |>
  mutate(offense_group = case_when(
    fbi_index %in% c("Murder or Nonnegligent Manslaughter",
                     "Negligent Manslaughter",
                     "Rape or Sexual Assault",
                     "Robbery",
                     "Aggravated or Simple Assault",
                     "Other Violent Offenses") ~ "Violent",
    fbi_index %in% c("Drug", "Public Order", "Property") ~ "Nonviolent",
    TRUE ~ fbi_index))

# Filter out "Unknown" offenses for further analysis
ncrp_past_pe_offense_filtered <- ncrp_past_pe_offense_initial |>
  filter(offense_group != "Unknown") |>
  fnc_filter_by_year(which_overall_year)

# Check if each state has all nonviolent offense types (Property, Drug, Public Order)
states_with_all_offenses <- ncrp_past_pe_offense_filtered |>
  filter(offense_group == "Nonviolent") |>
  group_by(state) |>
  summarise(has_property = any(fbi_index == "Property"),
            has_drug = any(fbi_index == "Drug"),
            has_public_order = any(fbi_index == "Public Order"),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  filter(has_property & has_drug & has_public_order) |>
  pull(state)

# Summarize counts for nonviolent offense subcategories
# Property, Drug, Public Order
nonviolent_summary <- ncrp_past_pe_offense_filtered |>
  filter(offense_group == "Nonviolent") |>
  group_by(fbi_index) |> 
  summarise(total_people = n(), .groups = "drop")

# Extract counts for subcategories

drug_offense_count <- nonviolent_summary$total_people[nonviolent_summary$fbi_index == "Drug"]
public_order_offense_count <- nonviolent_summary$total_people[nonviolent_summary$fbi_index == "Public Order"]

# Total nonviolent count and states with all offenses
nonviolent_offense_count <- sum(nonviolent_summary$total_people)
states_with_nonviolent <- length(states_with_all_offenses)

# Generate output sentence
cat("Over", highlight_number(comma(nonviolent_offense_count)), 
    "people held past their parole eligibility year were convicted of nonviolent offenses in",

title: Colorado
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
For most felonies in Colorado, people who are incarcerated become eligible for discretionary parole release after they have served 50 percent of the sentence imposed by the court. This eligibility formula assumes that a person in prison has earned all available good time credits of 15 days per month.²

title: Colorado
text:
Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.³ (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent). Second, the absolute min. sentence of 2 years, only when the most serious offense might be one with special eligibility restrictions. is computed. The previously computed rules-based estimate was replaced by the absolute min. sentence if the latter was. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁵

title: Colorado
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration rates past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Parole Release: This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Idaho
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined

Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Idaho, courts must specify a “minimum period of confinement” and may add a subsequent “indeterminate period” of the sentence. Courts have broad discretion over minimum terms, maximum sentences, and the ratio between the two. People who are incarcerated become parole eligible upon the expiration of the minimum sentence; that is, they become eligible on the date their indeterminate period begins.²

title: Idaho
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration rates past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Parole Release: This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Louisiana
text:
Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.³ (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (25 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 75 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁵

In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration rates past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Parole Release: This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

text:
Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.³ (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 50 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Data is excluded for years where more than 10 percent of the relevant cases are missing the admission year or the max. sentence length variables, and for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases areare missing PEY. Relevant cases are all except those with life sentences, sentences of less than a year, and admission types that are not new court commitments or not available. All figures are calculated among relevant cases only.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.

title: Tennessee
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Tennessee, sentences under two years are released to probation automatically upon eligibility. There is a statutory staggered percentage formula (generally 30–60 percent of the maximum sentence) for parole-release eligibility, differentiated by sentencing “range” based on prior convictions. Starting in 2021, there is a growing list of 100-percent offenses.²

title: Tennessee
text:
In Prison Past Parole Eligibility
Most recent data shows that 62 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, another 26 percent will reach parole eligibility next year, and an additional 12 percent will reach parole eligibility in more than one year.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 8 percent. Our forecasting model projects that the percentage of people past their initial parole eligibility will decrease by 1 percent.

title: Tennessee
text:
Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.³ (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (60 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 60 for those whose most serious offense is violent, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). Third, in Tennesee, the percentage of the max. sentence that corresponds to the min. sentence depends on previous sentences.Because data on previous terms was not published for 2018 and 2020, all cases were assumed to have previous sentences. Consequently, the rules-based estimated min. sentence was replaced by 60 percent of the max. sentence. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁵

Disparities can be measured in many different ways by comparing the outcomes of different groups. This page details disparities in three types of metrics by race, ethnicity, and sex:

title: Tennessee
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration rates past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Parole Release: This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.
Including ‘Other’ Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.
